notes: Scan produced 0 candidate keys; issues below were found via manual semantic comparison.

issues.subject: BH-C04 drain-on-drop: early-stop condition allowed vs forbidden

statement_a.excerpt:
- MUST NOT cancel the backend process/stream just because the universal receiver is dropped.
- MUST keep draining until backend stream ends (or until a defined “give up” condition that is explicitly justified; ADR-0013 implies full drain).

statement_b.excerpt: - Fully implement `BH-C04 stream forwarding + drain-on-drop` semantics in the harness pump: ... keep draining the backend stream until end regardless.

analysis.why_conflict: One statement explicitly allows a justified, explicit early-stop (“give up”) condition for draining; the other states draining must continue “until end regardless.” Under the same scope (“BH-C04 semantics”), both cannot be simultaneously true without an explicit scoping rule for when an early-stop is permitted.
possible_reconciliations: Make full drain unconditional by removing the “give up” allowance everywhere BH-C04 is described.
Keep a “give up” path, but define it concretely (trigger, guarantees preserved, and why it is safe), then update all BH-C04 statements to reflect the same scoped rule.
what_must_be_true: Whether BH-C04 permits any early-stop condition at all.
If early-stop is permitted: the exact stop condition(s) and the minimum guarantees that still hold (e.g., no deadlock/cancellation, completion signaling behavior, observability implications).

issues.subject: BH-C05 consumer-drop escape hatch vs harness driver ordering (pump/drain vs completion send)

statement_a.excerpt:
- Create: ... a harness driver task that runs the SEAM-3 pump and then sends completion.
- Return `build_gated_run_handle(rx, completion_rx)` as the only `AgentWrapperRunHandle` construction.

statement_b.excerpt: - **Consumer-drop path**: if the consumer drops the events stream, completion may resolve once the backend completion is ready (while draining continues in the background).

analysis.why_conflict: If “the SEAM-3 pump” is the unit that performs the full drain-to-end behavior, then “run the pump and then send completion” implies completion is only sent after draining has finished. That contradicts the consumer-drop requirement that completion may resolve once backend completion is ready while draining is still ongoing. The docs are missing a scope/definition of whether the ‘pump’ returns only after draining, or whether draining is performed by a background task that outlives completion sending.
possible_reconciliations: Define the driver as two coupled tasks: (1) a drain/forward task that runs to stream end, and (2) an independent completion task that sends completion once backend completion is ready; rely on `run_handle_gate` to control observability (finality-gated unless consumer dropped).

Redefine/clarify what “SEAM-3 pump” means in SEAM-4: if it can detach draining to a background task and return early (based on the eligibility rule), document that explicitly and ensure all call sites use the same lifecycle model.
what_must_be_true: Whether the SEAM-3 ‘pump’ returns only after backend stream end, or can return earlier while a background drainer continues.
When the completion oneshot is sent relative to stream draining and event-sender drop (the finality signal).
How the consumer-drop escape hatch is achieved without violating ‘keep draining’ guarantees.
